1:
The Chronicler's roll of the trans-Jordan tribes opens not with a name but with an apology, and the witnesses gather to explain why the firstborn is made to wait. The reformed John Gill notes the structure first: the sons of Reuben are announced here, but the list proper does not begin until v.3, for all that lies between is a parenthesis on why the birthright was lost. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke frames the same puzzle as a question — Reuben was the eldest son of Jacob, so why is his genealogy not reviewed first? — and answers that he forfeited the birthright by the transgression recorded in Gen. 35:22 and Gen. 49:4, the precedency passing to Judah, from whom the chief ruler came.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the demotion into a sermon on how sin lessens a man. Reuben might have challenged the precedency, but he defiled his father's concubine and was sentenced not to excel; sin thrusts men down from their excellency, and seventh-commandment sins especially leave an indelible stain upon a name, a reproach time will not wipe away, so that Reuben's seed to the last bear the disgrace of Reuben's sin. Yet Henry weighs the mercy against the judgment: though the tribe was degraded it was not discarded or disinherited, for the sullying of an Israelite's honour is not the losing of his happiness. And when the birthright was taken it did not simply devolve upon Simeon, the next in order; being typical, it followed not the course of nature but the choice of grace. Its two advantages, dominion and a double portion, were thought too much for any one head, and so were divided: Joseph received the double portion in his two tribes, Ephraim and Manasseh, each blessed by faith as a son (Heb. 11:21; Gen. 48:15-22), while Judah received the dominion, the sceptre entailed on him in Gen. 49:10, of whom came the chief ruler David and, in the fulness of time, Messiah the Prince (Mic. 5:2).

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the parenthesis as a deliberate defence of the house of David. It is, he says, an assertion of the legitimacy of the Davidic monarchy against the fact that both Reuben and Joseph had claims prior to Judah's; and he grounds the transfer in the very words of Jacob's blessing (Gen. 49:3) and curse. The reading of some manuscripts, with the Syriac and Arabic, gives the birthright "to Joseph," which he judges probably original, the double portion defined by the law of the hated wife's firstborn in Deut. 21:15-17. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley closes the circle: "sons of Israel" is added emphatically, he observes, because these sons were treated as if they had been the immediate sons of Jacob, and the whole order of the register is ruled not by birthright but by the higher privilege given to Judah. The witnesses do not contend; together they make Reuben's lost crown the frame of the chapter, a tribe kept in the record yet stripped of its precedence, that the throne of David might stand.
2:
The parenthesis deepens with its reason — "for Judah prevailed above his brethren" — and the witnesses divide their attention between the tribe's ascendancy and the ruler it produced. The reformed John Gill reads the prevailing plainly: the tribe of Judah excelled the rest in number, in valour and courage, and in dignity, so that the genealogy is reckoned not by birthright but by dominion, and for that reason the Chronicler had begun his tribal lists with Judah rather than Reuben. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat the verse as the hinge of the passage: after this parenthetic explanation the words "the sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel" are taken up again in v.3 and the four sons at last enumerated, names that answer exactly to the older registers in Gen. 46:9, Exod. 6:14, and Num. 26:5-7.

On the clause "of him came the chief ruler" the witnesses lift their eyes to a greater than David. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports that both the Syriac and Arabic understand it of Christ — "from Judah the King Messiah shall proceed" — and he gives at length the Chaldee paraphrase, which has the kingdom taken from Reuben and given to Judah for his strength, the high priesthood taken from Reuben's line and given to Aaron because Levi did not transgress in the matter of the golden calf, the custody of the sanctuary assigned to the Levites, and the birthright to Joseph. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads "prevailed" as literally "was mighty among his brethren," pointing to Jacob's blessing in Gen. 49:8-10 and to Judah's commission to lead the attack on the Canaanites in Judg. 1:1-2, and noting that at the census of Moses Judah greatly outnumbered any other tribe (Num. 1:27). The chief ruler, he says, is David, as in 1 Sam. 13:14, yet the prophecy is only exhausted of its meaning when we remember that our Lord sprang out of Judah (Heb. 7:14). The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds only the necessary caution, that it is not the person Judah but the tribe that prevailed, excelling the others in this one high privilege. The witnesses converge: the birthright was Joseph's, but the dominion was Judah's, and the register bends its whole order to honour the sceptre that would run down to the Messiah.
3:
With the apology finished the list itself begins, and the four sons of Reuben are set down: Hanoch, and Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi. The witnesses are brief, for the names are borrowed and known, but they do not pass them by without a word. The reformed John Gill simply anchors them to the older record, observing that they stand here as in the books of Moses, the Chronicler adding nothing but preserving the received succession. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the correspondence, that these four answer exactly to the lists in Gen. 46:9, Exod. 6:14, and Num. 26:5-7, where the same names appear as the heads of the Reubenite clans.

Ellicott lingers on one curiosity that a careless eye would miss. It is remarkable, he says, to find the names Hezron and Carmi recurring here among the main branches of Reuben, since both are also prominent among the clans of Judah — a small coincidence of names shared between the tribe that lost the birthright and the tribe that gained the dominion. He does not press it into a theory, but flags it as one of those places where the register rewards close attention, the same name serving two houses without implying one family.

The witnesses thus make even this bare verse a lesson in the Chronicler's method. He is not composing a fresh genealogy but copying a sacred one, link by link, and the four sons of Reuben are received exactly as the law had given them. That the historian takes such care to reproduce the old names unchanged — Hanoch and Pallu, Hezron and Carmi — is itself a testimony to the reverence with which he handled his sources; he adds no ornament, invents no descent, but sets down the four founders of the Reubenite clans and passes on to trace, through Joel, the princely line that will carry the tribe down to its captivity. In so borrowed a list the commentators find nothing to dispute and everything to preserve, the foundation stones of a tribe laid exactly where the earlier books had laid them.
4:
From the four sons the historian singles out one line to follow, the house descended through Joel, and the witnesses attend to where this Joel stands and where his line will end. The reformed John Gill weighs the parentage: Joel was either the son of Carmi, last named in the previous verse, or rather of Hanoch, Reuben's firstborn, since it was the descendants of this house who became the princes of the tribe. His posterity ran on in succession — Shemaiah, Gog, Shimei, Micah, Reaia, Baal, and Beerah — of whom, Gill admits frankly, we know no more than their bare names, and by these the descent is carried down to the deportation under Tiglath-pileser.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds a textual note from the Greek, observing that the Septuagint reads the pedigree a little differently, giving "the sons of Joel, Shemaiah, and Banaiah his son; and the sons of Gog, son of Shemaiah, his son Micah," and so on — a chain of "his son... his son" that arranges the same men into a slightly altered sequence. The variation touches the framing of the descent, not its substance; the succession of princes remains.

The witnesses feel the quiet weight of a list that is almost nothing but names. Here is a princely house of Reuben, several generations deep, and not a deed is recorded of any of them save the last, whose only notice is that he was carried away. The historian keeps the line precisely because it ended in exile; every name is a step toward the captivity that closes the chapter. Gill's candour — that we know no more than their names — is not a complaint but a recognition of the register's purpose: it does not celebrate these men, it numbers them, holding the thread of the tribe unbroken from Joel down to the prince who lost both throne and land to Assyria. The very anonymity of the line, deed-less and silent, throws all the weight forward onto its ending, where the last name will be met by the name of the king who deported him.
6:
The princely line reaches its last link, and the witnesses pause where the tribe's independent history stops — "Beerah his son, whom Tilgath-pilneser carried away." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws out the significance of the notice: after the ten tribes broke away from the house of David they continued to have princes of their own, and this continued until Tiglath-pileser carried them captive into Assyria. At that hour Beerah was their prince or chief, and with him, Clarke observes, this whole species of dominion or precedency in Reuben came to an end. He reports too the tradition of the Targum, which identifies Beerah with Baruch the prophet.

The reformed John Gill gathers a different tradition and then sets it aside. According to the Targum and other Jewish writers, he notes, this Beerah was a prophet and the father of Hosea; but Gill will not have it, for neither the name, nor the title, nor the time, nor the tribe agree — a sober refusal to fasten a famous name onto a stranger merely because the rabbis wished it so. The two witnesses thus stand together against the impulse to make Beerah more than he was, whether Baruch or the father of a prophet.

What matters, both agree, is not who Beerah might be imagined to be but what happened to him. He is named at all only because he is the man in whose day the line of Reubenite princes was extinguished, led away by the Assyrian king whose name will toll again at the chapter's close. The register that began with the lost birthright now reaches a lost princedom; the tribe that Jacob sentenced not to excel is followed down through its quiet generations to the very prince who watched it carried into exile. Beerah's single deed, if it can be called one, is to be deported, and the historian records him as the full stop at the end of Reuben's long, diminished sentence — the last of the princes, remembered for nothing but the captivity that swallowed his people. Clarke and Gill together let the verse stand as the tribe's quiet epitaph: a prince named only that his deportation might be dated, the once-royal firstborn of Israel handed, without a struggle recorded, into the keeping of Assyria.
7:
Beside the princely line the register sets the heads of the other Reubenite houses — "his brethren by their families" — and the witnesses work to sort out who these kinsmen were. The reformed John Gill offers two readings without deciding between them: the brethren may be the actual brothers of Beerah, or more loosely the rest of the posterity of Reuben reckoned alongside him. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses for precision, rendering the phrase "his fellow-tribesmen, each after his clan, in the registration according to their pedigrees," and identifying the chiefs named as Jeiel and Zechariah, with Bela following.

Ellicott finds in these names a hidden order. Jeiel, he explains, was the chief of the second Reubenite clan, as Beerah had been of the first, while Zechariah and Bela headed the remaining chief houses; and these four chieftains correspond precisely to the four divisions of the tribe listed in v.3. He supports the scheme from the law, where the muster of Reuben in Num. 26:7 names exactly "the Hanochite, the Palluite, the Hezronite, and the Carmite" as the clans of the Reubenite, and where the arrangement of each man after his clan is the very method commanded in Num. 2:34.

The witnesses thus read the verse as more than a scatter of names. Behind the chiefs Jeiel and Zechariah and Bela stands the old four-fold division of Reuben, the tribe registered by its ancient clans exactly as Moses had numbered it in the wilderness. Gill's caution and Ellicott's system are not at odds; both recognize that the Chronicler is preserving a genuine tribal structure, not a random genealogy. The house of Beerah led the registration, but it did not stand alone, and the historian is careful to set the fellow-chiefs beside the prince, so that the whole tribe of Reuben, in all its families, is accounted for before he turns eastward to trace how far their herds and their conquests had carried them. The verse, bare as it looks, thus completes the muster of Reuben, every clan and chief set in its place, so that not a family of the firstborn tribe is left uncounted before the narrative moves from pedigree to the wars and wanderings that follow.
8:
One of those chief houses is now traced in detail, the house of Bela, and with it the register turns from names to territory. The reformed John Gill follows the pedigree upward — Bela the son of Azaz, the son of Shema, the son of Joel — noting that Shema is reckoned by Kimchi and Ben Melech to be the same as the Shemaiah named earlier, so that Bela's line reaches back to the same Joel who fathered the princes. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott cautions against pressing that identity too hard: Bela's descent is traced, like Beerah's, but through fewer names, and this does not prove the two men were not contemporaries, since intermediate links are often dropped in genealogies, as the varying lengths of the pedigrees in 1 Chron. 6:33-47 and the compressed line of Achan in Josh. 7:18 both show. Nor, he adds, is the Joel of this verse necessarily the Joel of v.4, for all the coincidence of a Shema and a Shemaiah.

On the places the witnesses grow specific and vivid. Bela dwelt "in Aroer, even unto Nebo and Baal-meon," and the commentators plant each name on the map. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sets Aroer on the river Arnon and marks Nebo as both a city and a mountain in the same country, all of it lying on the far side of Jordan. Ellicott is finer still: Aroer is the modern Ara'ir on the north bank of the Arnon (Josh. 12:2); Nebo is the place on the famous mount east of the Dead Sea, over against Jericho (Num. 32:38; Deut. 34:1); Baal-meon, or Beth-baal-meon, is now Ma'in, a little south-east of Heshbon. Aroer gives the southern limit and Nebo the northern, he explains, so that the three towns fix the borders of Bela's holding — and all three, he notes tellingly, are named on the Stone of Mesha king of Moab (2 Kings 3:4-27), the very land Moab would later reclaim.

The witnesses read the verse, then, as the register turning into geography. Keil and Delitzsch join the note to the verse that follows, remarking that Bela's family had spread so far because their herds were numerous in the land of Gilead. The house that began as a name in a pedigree becomes a people filling towns from the Arnon to Nebo, and the historian's careful siting of Aroer and Baal-meon is his way of showing how a tribe of the birthright-less firstborn nonetheless grew and pressed outward across the whole trans-Jordan.
9:
The verse follows Bela's house eastward until it runs up against the desert, and the witnesses read the expansion as both a boast and, for a later reader, a wound. The reformed John Gill notes only the ambiguity of the subject — "eastward he inhabited" may mean Bela himself or the tribe of Reuben at large — but the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fills in the movement: as their flocks and herds increased, the Reubenites gradually spread toward the great desert lying between Syria and the Euphrates, driven outward by the sheer multiplication of their cattle in the land of Gilead, which in Old Testament usage means all the Israelite territory east of Jordan.

Ellicott hears in the wording a clue to when the account was written. The phrase "unto the entrance into the wilderness from the river Euphrates," he suggests, seems to indicate that this record was composed originally in Babylonia, by a hand that measured the desert from the great river's side; and he recalls that this waste was a painful memory to the restored exiles, for Ezra took four months to cross it (Ezra 7:9; Ezra 8:22). So the very geography of Reuben's growth carries the scent of the exile that will end the chapter.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley guards against overstating the reach. Reuben's border ran from Jordan and the wilderness beyond it toward the Euphrates, he grants, but not to the river itself, to which their habitation never actually reached; the phrase means the great wilderness of Kedemoth (Deut. 2:26), which stretched far and wide in the Euphrates' direction, and their being forced to enlarge their dwellings as far as they could was simply the pressure of their multiplied flocks. The witnesses converge: the tribe spread as its herds demanded, filling the eastern country to the edge of the desert but not beyond, a prosperity that looked like blessing and yet planted them, exposed, on the very frontier down which the Assyrian would one day come. Ellicott's guess that the account was penned in Babylonia only deepens the irony the witnesses feel: the very record of how far Reuben's herds had carried them was written, it seems, by a hand already in the exile toward which that expansion tended.
10:
The eastward spread turns to conquest, and the verse records the first of the chapter's two wars with the Hagarites, fought "in the days of Saul." The witnesses first settle who these Hagarites were. The reformed John Gill dismisses the Targum's fancy that they were Hungarians, a people not then in being, and identifies them rather as Ishmaelites, so called from Hagar, Sarah's maid — the same tribe that Pliny and Ptolemy place in Arabia near the men of Bashan. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke pictures their manner of life: they were nomad, tent-dwelling Arabs with no fixed homes, whose whole property was in cattle, so that the Reubenites, having beaten them, seized their tents and their herds and dwelt in their place. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the name from the Psalter, where they appear as the Hagrim of Ps. 83:7, and notes that this Arab nation is named in the Assyrian inscriptions, the very phrase "they dwelt in their tents" echoing the ancient words of Gen. 9:27.

Beneath the agreement runs a quiet difference over who exactly waged the war. Gill reads it broadly, of the Reubenites acting in concert with the Gadites and the half-tribe of Manasseh, and even ventures a date — perhaps about the time Saul relieved Jabesh-gilead and beat the Ammonites, which may have emboldened the tribes across Jordan. Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch read it more narrowly: the great eastward extension belonged to Bela and his clan, the Belaites who fought the Hagarites for the pasture-grounds east of Gilead. Keil weighs the grammar carefully — the singular used of Bela in the previous verses gives way here to a plural — and, though he grants the words might be referred to the whole tribe of Reuben, he judges it better to refer them to Bela's family, whose wide spread had just been described.

The witnesses do not quarrel over the outcome, only over its agents. Ellicott takes Jetur and the sons of Ishmael (Gen. 25:15) as the kindred of these Hagarites, and all agree that the victory carried the conquerors into the enemy's tents "throughout all the east land of Gilead," or rather, as Ellicott renders it, over the whole eastern border of Gilead. Whether the triumph belonged to Bela's single clan or to the united tribe, it marks the high tide of Reuben's fortunes — the same border, won by the sword in Saul's day, that the tribe would lose forever to a greater eastern power at the chapter's end.
11:
The register crosses from Reuben to Gad, and the witnesses mark first the neighbourly arrangement of the two tribes. The reformed John Gill reads "over against them" as meaning the Gadites dwelt beside the Reubenites, one part of Gilead falling to Gad between the two, the other part to the half-tribe of Manasseh. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the clause more exactly — "over against them dwelt the Gadites in Bashan unto Salcah" — and fix the geography with care.

Bashan, they explain, is used here in its wider sense, for the whole dominion of King Og, which embraced both the northern half of Gilead lying above the Jabbok and the district of Bashan proper, as the law describes it in Deut. 3:10. This was no vague upland but the old kingdom Israel had won by conquest east of Jordan, and Gad now held its southern reaches. Salcah formed the eastern boundary, the town they identify with the modern Szalchad, some six hours' journey eastward from Bosra; so Gad's holding is drawn from the Jabbok northward and out to the desert's edge.

The witnesses treat the verse as the header to Gad's whole section, laying down the land before naming the men. Where Reuben had spread southward and eastward from the Arnon, Gad is set to the north, over against its brother-tribe, holding Bashan to Salcah. Gill's simpler note and Keil's precise mapping agree in substance: the two-and-a-half tribes are being settled, each in its portion, across the trans-Jordan that Moses had granted them. The care with which the historian and his interpreters fix these borders — Aroer and Nebo for Reuben, Bashan and Salcah for Gad — is not idle, for it is exactly this hard-won eastern land, lying most exposed to the nations, that the chapter will show swept away. The map is drawn in full just before it is erased, and the reader who knows the ending feels, even in this quiet notice of a boundary between brother-tribes, the first faint shadow of the loss to come. Gill and Keil, the one brief and the other exact, leave Gad settled over against Reuben in the wide realm of Og, the tribe's dwelling fixed before a single one of its chiefs is named.
12:
The heads of Gad are numbered, and the witnesses read the short roll of chiefs against the tribe's older records. The reformed John Gill notes simply that these were the principal men of Gad, the chief among them a Joel — a different Joel, he is careful to say, from the one who headed the line in Reuben. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain why the list opens with chiefs rather than with the tribe's founding sons: the sons of Gad given in Gen. 46:16 are not repeated here, because the enumeration of Gad's families was already introduced in the previous verse, and the genealogical thread connecting these later chiefs to the ancestral sons had not been preserved.

Keil sorts the names into their ranks. The four here — Joel the chief, Shapham the second, then Jaanai and Shaphat "in Bashan" — are the heads of fathers'-houses dwelling in that district; and the seven names that follow in the next verse are their brethren, a further set of chiefs reckoned by their fathers'-houses. The historian, that is, is giving the leadership of Gad in two ranks, the principal four and the seven beside them.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke brings in the Targum's characteristic embellishment, which cannot leave a bare title alone: it makes Joel "prince of the Sanhedrin," Shapham "master of the college," and Jaanai and Shaphat "judges in Mathnan," dressing the old tribal chiefs in the robes of a later Jewish polity. Clarke reports the gloss for what it is, an anachronistic flourish rather than history. The witnesses thus leave the verse as a faithful muster of Gad's leading houses, the names received from an authentic register even where the line back to the patriarch Gad had been lost — one more sign of the Chronicler's fidelity to his materials, keeping what he had and inventing nothing to fill what he lacked. The four chiefs in Bashan and the seven who follow are received simply as the leadership of Gad in its appointed land, the tribe mustered by its heads before its dwellings and its deeds are told. In this the witnesses see the Chronicler's constant care, to preserve a true record even in its gaps, naming the chiefs he could and confessing the link he could not supply.
13:
The seven brother-chiefs of Gad are named — Michael, and Meshullam, and Sheba, and Jorai, and Jachan, and Zia, and Heber — and the witnesses receive them as a genuine, if bare, register. The reformed John Gill remarks only that these too were men of eminence and note in the tribe, heads of houses beside the four already listed. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set them in their place: they are the brethren, according to their fathers'-houses, of the chiefs named in the previous verse, and their own genealogy is given in the verses that follow, tracing them up through Abihail to Buz and their common head Ahi.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds a small textual observation, that this whole verse is wanting in both the Syriac and the Arabic versions — dropped, perhaps, as a mere list of unfamiliar names by translators who saw little to keep in it. Clarke notes the omission without alarm, one of the ordinary variants between the versions that the chapter has met before.

The witnesses make of the seven names a quiet testimony to the completeness of the record. The historian does not content himself with the four principal chiefs but numbers their brethren too, house by house, so that the leadership of Gad is set down in full. That such obscure names — of whom, as with Reuben's princes, nothing is known but the names themselves — should be preserved at all is itself the point the commentators feel: the Chronicler is keeping an inventory of the covenant people, and no head of a father's-house is too small to be counted. The seven brethren of Gad stand in the sacred register not for any recorded deed but simply as members of the tribe, numbered and held against the day when their whole society across the Jordan would be carried away and this list would be all that remained of them. That the Chronicler should preserve, house by house, names of which nothing else is known is the very mark of his purpose, to keep the covenant people remembered even in the generation that would be lost.
14:
The pedigree of the seven brethren is given, carried up from Abihail through his fathers, and the witnesses attend to the unusual direction in which it runs. The reformed John Gill traces the ascent plainly: the seven were the children of Abihail, whose line is followed back through Huri, Jaroah, Gilead, Michael, Jeshishai, and Jahdo to Buz — a genealogy climbing from son to father rather than descending from father to son.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already noticed the reversal and drawn a small lesson from it. In this tribe of Gad, he observes, the pedigree of Abihail's children is carried upwards, from the son to the father, whereas the princely line of Reuben in v.4 was brought downwards, from father to son — the register varying its manner as its sources allowed, ascending here and descending there but keeping the succession either way. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch handle these verses as a single block, following the same chain from Abihail to Buz and on to Ahi the son of Abdiel, the head of these fathers'-houses, whom they conjecture to have lived in the days of Jotham of Judah or Jeroboam of Israel, when, as the following verses record, the genealogies of Gad were drawn up.

The witnesses thus read the verse as a genuine fragment of tribal record, precise in its chain of fathers even where it tells us nothing of the men. That the historian could carry Abihail's line back through so many generations, naming each, shows how full the archives of Gad once were; and Henry's point about the upward reckoning reminds the reader that these lists were living documents, compiled from real family registers, not schematic inventions. The bare names of Huri and Jaroah and Jahdo are received, like the seven brethren they explain, as authentic links in the ordered memory of a tribe soon to lose everything but its name in the register. The pedigree is offered not as antiquarian ornament but as evidence, the deep and datable descent of houses whose only monument would be this careful chain of fathers. Gill, Henry, and Keil alike treat the ascending list not as a tangle to be unravelled but as a memory to be kept, each obscure father a genuine link between Abihail's sons and the tribe's remembered past.
15:
The genealogy comes to its head in Ahi, the son of Abdiel, the son of Guni, "chief of the house of their fathers," and the witnesses read him as the man in whom the whole company of Gadite brethren was gathered. The reformed John Gill explains the office: Ahi was the principal man of the families to which the seven brethren belonged, their head and representative, though besides him each of them was in turn the head of his own house — a leadership ranked, chief over chiefs.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, treating the pedigree as one piece, fix the same Ahi as the head of the fathers'-houses named in the preceding verses, and venture a date for him. He is conjectured, they say, to have lived in the time of King Jotham of Judah, or of Jeroboam of Israel — the two reigns under which, according to v.17, the genealogical registers of the Gadites were made up. Ahi thus stands at the point where the family record was actually compiled, the living chief whose generation set down its own descent.

The witnesses leave the verse as the capstone of Gad's genealogy. Where Abihail's line climbed backward into the past, Ahi anchors it in a datable present, the head under whom the tribe's houses were counted and enrolled. Gill's careful ranking — Ahi supreme, the others each a head beneath him — and Keil's dating agree in making this obscure name the hinge between the ancient succession and the historical moment of registration. It is a small thing to be "chief of the house of their fathers," and Scripture tells us nothing more of the man; yet the Chronicler keeps him, because it was in his day and under his headship that the memory of Gad was gathered into the record that alone survived the tribe. The genealogy ends, fittingly, at the man who made the genealogy, the chief whose act of registration preserved the very names we read. So Gill's ranking and Keil's dating converge on a single figure, at once the summit of the pedigree and the hand under which it was written down, the last name of the list and the reason the list exists at all.
16:
The dwelling-places of Gad are gathered into a single sentence — Gilead, Bashan and her towns, and the pasture-lands of Sharon — and the witnesses divide over the last of these names. The reformed John Gill notes plainly that they dwelt in that part of Gilead which belonged to Gad. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch expand the whole: the families descended from Buz dwelt in Gilead, in the part south of the Jabbok that Moses had given to Gad and Reuben (Deut. 3:12), and in Bashan and her "daughters," the villages belonging to its cities, and in all the pasture-grounds — the same word for common land used in Num. 35:2 — of Sharon, out to their farthest borders.

But which Sharon? Here the witnesses genuinely part. Keil and Delitzsch take it to be the famous Sharon, the great plain not in Perea at all but along the shore of the Mediterranean, running from Carmel to near Joppa, celebrated for its fertility and its flowers in Song 2:1, Isa. 33:9, Isa. 35:2, and Isa. 65:10; Jerome, they recall, called all the land from Caesarea to Joppa "Saronas." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke resolves the difficulty differently, by distinguishing three places of the name: one a district in Bashan beyond Jordan (Josh. 12:18), one between Caesarea and Joppa, and a third between Mount Tabor and the Sea of Tiberias. On Clarke's reckoning the Sharon of Gad is the trans-Jordan district, not the coastal plain at all.

So the debate is real, and turns on geography: is the Sharon where Gad pastured its flocks the great western plain, oddly reached across the Jordan, or a separate eastern Sharon in Bashan bearing the same name? Keil holds to the one famous Sharon; Clarke multiplies the name to keep Gad's holding wholly in the east. The witnesses do not resolve it, and the very difficulty illustrates the hazard of these registers, where a single place-name may belong to two or three regions at once. What is not in doubt is the picture of a tribe rich in cattle spreading over a broad grazing country — Gilead and Bashan and the meadows of Sharon, wherever exactly that Sharon lay — filling the trans-Jordan to its borders in the last generations before the exile.
17:
The genealogies of Gad are dated — "in the days of Jotham king of Judah, and in the days of Jeroboam king of Israel" — and the witnesses find in that double dating both a chronological puzzle and a providence. The reformed John Gill notes only that all the foregoing were reckoned by these registers. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch face the difficulty that the two kings did not reign together: Jotham came to the throne of Judah some twenty-five years after Jeroboam of Israel had died. There must therefore have been two distinct registrations, and Jotham's is named first because Judah held the legitimate kingship; that made under Jeroboam was probably undertaken after he had restored the ancient boundaries of Israel, as 2 Kings 14:25 records. Keil grants that Jotham could have enrolled the Gadites only if part of the trans-Jordan had come for a time under his dominion — for which there is no direct record, though after the anarchy that followed Jeroboam's death it is not unlikely.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts his eyes from the chronology to the timing's deeper irony. These genealogies, he observes, were perfected under Jotham but begun years earlier under Jeroboam the Second, just before the tribes were carried away by the Assyrians, as may be seen in 2 Kings 15:29. It is a solemn thing, he says, that when the judgments of God were ready to break out against them for their apostasy, then were they priding themselves on their pedigrees, that they were the children of the covenant — just as the Jews of our Saviour's day, when ripe for ruin, boasted "we have Abraham to our father."

Yet Henry will not let the notice end in reproach. There might, he adds, be a special providence in it, a favourable intimation that though the tribes were for the present cast out, they were not cast off for ever; what we design to call for hereafter, we keep an inventory of. The witnesses thus read the verse two ways at once: as a mustering of Gad's houses on the very eve of their ruin, and as a quiet pledge that the God who kept their names in a book had not finally forgotten them. The register that seems only to count the doomed may also be preserving them for a mercy not yet revealed.
18:
The narrative now gathers all two-and-a-half tribes into a single host — "valiant men, able to bear buckler and sword, and to shoot with bow, and skilful in war, four and forty thousand seven hundred and threescore" — and the witnesses admire both the muster and the reason for its record. The reformed John Gill notes simply that these tribes, dwelling together on the one side of Jordan, joined their forces as one. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to the placement of the account, observing that since the half-tribe of Manasseh shares in this war, one might have expected the story after v.24; its position here, they judge, is owing not to some artificial symmetry but to the character of the sources the Chronicler was following.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the army the occasion for a meditation on holy war. What a brave force these frontier-tribes brought into the field, he exclaims — forty-four thousand strong, skilful, effective men — and then adds the wistful thought: how much more considerable might Israel have been in the days of the judges if all the tribes had acted in conjunction. But his deeper interest is in how so small a company engaged so great a God. They cried to God and put their trust in him, and in that, Henry says, they acted as Israelites indeed. As the seed of believing Abraham they trusted not in their powerful army but in the divine power, leaning on the commission God had given them to war for the enlarging of their coasts (Deut. 20:15); and as the seed of praying Jacob they cried to God in the very heat of the battle, as Judah would later do against Jeroboam in 2 Chron. 13:14.

Henry draws the lesson forward to the reader. In distress God expects us to cry to him — he distrains upon us for this tribute; and in our spiritual conflicts we must look up to heaven for strength, for it is the believing prayer that will be the prevailing prayer. The witnesses thus refuse to let the muster-roll be merely military arithmetic. The forty-four thousand are counted, yes, but the point of the counting is that a well-armed and courageous host still won nothing by its own strength; it prayed, and trusted, and was heard. Keil's care for the record and Henry's care for the soul meet in the same verse: an army fully equipped for war, whose real weapon was the God it called upon.
19:
The enemy is named more fully now — "the Hagarites, with Jetur, and Nephish, and Nodab" — and the witnesses first identify these desert confederates and then dispute whether this is a new war or the old one retold. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch gather the names from Scripture and the classics: the Hagarites, contracted to the Hagrim of Ps. 83:7, are the Agraioi whom Strabo, on the authority of Eratosthenes, describes as leading a nomad life in the great Arabian desert; Jetur, father of the Itureans, and Nephish are Ishmaelites of the line in Gen. 25:15; and Nodab, named here only, is a Bedouin tribe of whom nothing else is known.

On the timing the witnesses divide. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke takes this to be probably the same war already mentioned in v.10, the account simply resumed and expanded, and adds that the Targum everywhere renders the Hagarites as "Hongarites." Keil and Delitzsch flatly disagree: the war in Saul's day was real enough, but this one is certainly of a later time, connected with the earlier only in that both sprang from similar causes. The clash, they infer from v.22, broke out at some point before the trans-Jordan tribes were carried into the Assyrian exile, but its exact date is not given. The reformed John Gill, for his part, simply refers the Hagarites back to those before mentioned, without pressing the question of whether one war or two is meant.

The disagreement is not idle. If Clarke is right, the chapter tells one Hagarite war twice, once in brief and once at length; if Keil is right, the trans-Jordan tribes fought the same restless desert peoples in two separate generations, the victory of Saul's day repeated long after. The witnesses leave the question open, but Keil's reading fits the flow of the narrative, which has moved from Saul's reign through the registrations of Jotham and Jeroboam before reaching this campaign. Either way, the roll of enemies — Hagarites, Jetur, Nephish, Nodab — sets the scene for the great deliverance the next verses describe, when a praying army overthrew a confederacy of the desert and, for a season, possessed its land.
20:
The battle is won, and the verse tells exactly how — "they were helped against them... for they cried to God in the battle, and he was intreated of them, because they put their trust in him." The witnesses fasten on that chain of cause and effect. The reformed John Gill weighs the source of the help: the Israelites were aided against the Ishmaelites either by their brethren of the house of Israel on the near side of Jordan, as the Targum supposes, or rather, he judges, by the Lord himself, to whom they cried.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the grammar as pointing wholly to God: it was "helped to them," that is, by God against the Hagarites and their allies, and "he was entreated of them because they trusted in him." From the very intensity of the language they infer how serious the war had been, a struggle in which the possession of the land itself was at stake; for the trans-Jordan tribes lived by their herds, and the desert peoples on their frontier were shepherds too, so that a quarrel over pasture could easily become a war of extermination. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley states the same conviction in a word: they were helped by God, who gave them extraordinary courage and success; and Adam Clarke notes that the Targum, characteristically, has them trust not merely in God but "in his Word."

The witnesses converge on the theology the verse presses so plainly. A great army had marched out in v.18, but the victory is credited not to its numbers or its skill but to its prayer and its trust. Gill's hesitation over whether men or God supplied the help only sharpens the point at which he arrives — that it was God, entreated in the thick of the fighting; and Keil's sober picture of a life-or-death struggle over grazing land shows how much these tribes had to lose and how little their own strength could secure it. The lesson the whole chapter will invert is written here in its brightest form: while they cried to God, they conquered. The tragedy of v.25 is that the same tribes would one day turn from the God who had heard them here.
21:
The spoils of the victory are counted out in round numbers — fifty thousand camels, two hundred and fifty thousand sheep, two thousand asses, and a hundred thousand captives — and the witnesses read the great tally as both the measure of the triumph and a moral problem. The reformed John Gill notes plainly that the conquerors took the cattle the Hagarites had brought with them, found in their camp when they fled or left in their fields. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch dwell on the sheer scale of the plunder, comparing it to the rich booty Israel took in the war against Midian in Num. 31:11-32, and treating the huge, rounded figures as the token of an overwhelming defeat.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke raises the harder question the numbers force. This, he says, was a war of extermination as to the political existence of the people, and nothing could justify such a war but the express direction of God — a direction he would never give against any nation until the cup of its iniquity was full. And here, Clarke insists, it was full: the Hagarites were sunk in idolatry, as the chapter itself will declare, so that their destruction fell under the same divine justice that had once swept the Canaanites from the land. The booty, on his reading, is not mere plunder but the visible sign of a judgment sanctioned from heaven.

The witnesses thus hold together the glory and the gravity of the conquest. The camels and the sheep and the hundred thousand persons are counted, as any victor counts his gains; yet Clarke will not let the reader glory in the slaughter without reckoning its ground, and Keil's comparison with the Midianite war sets it in the line of Israel's holy wars rather than of common raiding. The wealth that flowed into the trans-Jordan tribes from this victory was real, and it settled them richly in the enemy's country; but it came, the witnesses remind us, only because the war was of God — a phrase the very next verse will make explicit, and on which the whole justice of the day depends.
22:
The verse gives the reason for the slaughter and the sequel — "for there fell down many slain, because the war was of God: and they dwelt in their steads until the captivity" — and the witnesses read in it both a vindication and a warning. The reformed John Gill infers from the many slain how large the Hagarite army must have been, since so great a number fell besides all who were taken prisoner. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain that the rich booty of the previous verse need not surprise us, for the enemy had suffered a thoroughly bloody defeat; and the phrase "the war was from God" means that it was he who brought it to this result, exactly as in 2 Chron. 25:20 and 1 Sam. 17:47. Then they dwelt in the enemy's place, possessing the pasture-grounds that had been the Arabs' until they themselves were carried away by the Assyrians.

The wesleyan-arminian voices add their glosses. Adam Clarke suggests the hundred thousand were probably made slaves rather than slain, and notes that the Targum renders "the war was of God" as "from the Word of the Lord." John Wesley draws the practical moral: the war was of God because it was undertaken in his fear and carried on in dependence upon him, and then, he says, we may expect to prosper in any enterprise, and then only, when we take God along with us.

The witnesses feel the quiet menace in the verse's last clause. The victory was God's, the land was theirs, and they settled in it — but only "until the captivity." Keil marks the phrase as pointing forward to v.26, so that the very sentence recording their conquest already names its expiry. The tribes that dwelt in the tents of the Hagarites by the help of God would themselves be driven from those tents by the Assyrian, when they had turned from the God who gave the victory. Wesley's rule cuts both ways: they prospered while they took God along with them, and the day they left him behind, the borrowed land was lost. The triumph and the exile are bound together in a single verse.
23:
The register turns to the third of the trans-Jordan tribes, the half of Manasseh, and the witnesses trace how far north its multiplying families spread. The reformed John Gill sets them not in the land of the conquered Hagarites but in the Gilead and Bashan beyond Jordan that Moses had assigned them, the historian pausing to give a short account of their principal men. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry marks the growth: their lot at first was Bashan only, but they increased so much in wealth and power that they spread far to the north, even unto Hermon.

The grammatical-historical witnesses map the expansion precisely. Keil and Delitzsch note that the half-tribe was very numerous, dwelling in the land of Bashan and reaching northward to Baal-hermon, which they locate, following Josh. 12:7 and Josh. 13:5, in the valley of Lebanon under Mount Hermon, probably the modern Banias at the mountain's foot (Num. 34:8); and on to Senir and Mount Hermon itself. Senir, they explain, was the Amorite name of Hermon in Deut. 3:9, here and in Ezek. 27:5 used of a particular part of the range, as "Mount Hermon" names another. Charles Ellicott agrees, taking Baal-hermon to be perhaps the same as Baal-gad, the modern Banias, and noting that Senir's principal summit is now called the "hill of the chief" and "Snow Hill."

The witnesses read the verse as the last and northernmost reach of Israel's trans-Jordan settlement. Where Reuben held the south by the Arnon and Gad the centre in Bashan, Manasseh ran up to the very roots of Hermon, the snowy sentinel at the land's northern edge. The tribe's increase "from Bashan unto Baal-hermon" is recorded as a real prosperity, a people grown great and spread wide over the high northern country. Yet the reader who has followed the chapter knows what this fullness portends: the more widely these tribes spread across the exposed eastern and northern frontier, the more of them lay open to the power that would presently sweep down from the north and carry the whole borderland away. Gill, Henry, Keil, and Ellicott together trace the tribe from its first lot in Bashan to its farthest reach at Hermon, a growth recorded, the witnesses feel, chiefly to measure the height from which it would shortly fall.
24:
The chiefs of the half-tribe are named — Epher, and Ishi, and Eliel, and Azriel, and Jeremiah, and Hodaviah, and Jahdiel — and the witnesses note both their stature and the gap in the record where the list begins. The reformed John Gill remarks simply that these were some of the principal men of the half-tribe of Manasseh. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch count them as seven heads of fathers'-houses, characterized as valiant heroes and famous men, and observe that the enumeration begins oddly with the conjunction "and Epher," as though a name had dropped out before it; of these men, they add, nothing further has been handed down than the names.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott makes that very poverty of information eloquent. Of these seven "valiant warriors, men of renown, heads for their clans," nothing more is recorded; yet the meagre memorial of their names, he says, has at least this value — it proves that abundant materials for the history of Israel once existed, of which our canonical books have preserved only authentic fragments. The lost name at the head of the list and the seven that survive are, on Ellicott's reading, a window onto a whole vanished archive.

The witnesses thus turn a broken verse into a testimony. That the register opens mid-phrase, a name evidently fallen away, does not shake their confidence in what remains; both Keil and Ellicott treat the flaw honestly, as the ordinary damage of hand-copied texts, and Ellicott draws from it the reassuring inference that behind these fragments lay full records now lost. Seven mighty men of Manasseh are named and no more, their valour asserted but their deeds untold — and the very brevity, the witnesses feel, marks the point in the chapter where the tribes stand at their height, renowned and numerous, just before the historian turns from their glory to the apostasy and the exile that will end them all. Gill names them as princes, Keil and Ellicott as heroes and men of renown; and where the text itself has lost a name, the commentators find not a defect to hide but a proof that the Chronicler wrote from records once fuller than anything now left to us, preserving faithfully even the fragment that reached him.
25:
The chapter's long ascent breaks here into its fall — "they transgressed against the God of their fathers, and went a whoring after the gods of the people of the land, whom God destroyed before them" — and the witnesses read the verdict as the pivot on which everything turns. The reformed John Gill spreads the guilt across all three tribes: it was not the half-tribe of Manasseh only but the Reubenites and Gadites also who transgressed against God's law, his will, his word, and his ordinances. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch agree that the subject is the whole two-and-a-half tribes, clear from the next verse, and that these fell away faithlessly and went after the gods of the very peoples — the Amorites and Canaanites — whom God had destroyed before them.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke turns back to the recent victory to find its hidden justice. Now we see, he says, why God delivered the Hagarites into the hands of these tribes: the desert people were abominable idolaters, and therefore God destroyed them — the same judgment that now hangs over the conquerors, who have taken up the idols for which their enemies fell. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the language of the charge. To "transgress," he explains, is to be faithless or untrue to God, the word used of Achan's trespass in Josh. 7:1; and the figure of whoredom is exact, for Jehovah was the true Lord and Husband of Israel, so that apostasy from him is, in the prophets' language, adultery. The nation's fatal sin, he notes, showed itself in the worship of the high places, in the adoration of the heavenly bodies and the powers of nature, and in the practice of magic and divination.

Ellicott then lifts the verse to the Chronicler's whole vision of history. The peoples "whom God had destroyed before them" were reduced, to the historian's mind, by a divine work — every incident of the conquest the Lord's doing, whether through sun and moon or storm or the swords of Joshua's men, as Num. 21:21-35, Josh. 12:6, and Ps. 135:10-12 attest. And from the same standpoint the Chronicler will trace the coming Assyrian invasions to a direct impulse from the God of Israel; the Assyrian kings might credit their gods Ishtar and Bel, but they could not rise to the prophets' higher vision of one guiding power at the summit of the world (Isa. 10:5-15). The witnesses converge on the terrible symmetry: the tribes had watched God destroy the idolatrous nations before them and take their land; now, worshipping those same gods, they stand where their victims stood, and the God who fought for them will hand them, in turn, to the destroyer.
26:
The judgment falls, and the chapter ends where the whole trans-Jordan story has been tending — "the God of Israel stirred up the spirit of Pul king of Assyria, and the spirit of Tilgath-pilneser king of Assyria, and he carried them away." The witnesses read first the divine hand behind the imperial one. The reformed John Gill notes that the Targum, reverent of the name, reads "the Word of the God of Israel"; and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gathers the parallels for that stirring of a heathen king's spirit — the same phrase used of God's moving the Philistines in 2 Chron. 21:16 and of his waking the spirit of Cyrus in Ezra 1:1 and Isa. 44:28-45:1. The Assyrian marches at his own ambition, but it is the God of Israel who sets him in motion against a faithless people.

Over the two royal names the witnesses divide. Keil and Delitzsch read them as two distinct kings — Pul as the first Assyrian who attacked Israel, in the days of Menahem (2 Kings 15:19), and Tiglath-pileser as the one who actually led the East-Jordan tribes into exile (2 Kings 15:29). Ellicott argues rather for their identity: no trace of a Pul distinct from Tiglath-pileser has been found in the contemporary Assyrian monuments, and since that king actually claims the tribute of Menahem, Pul appears to have been his original name, discarded on his accession in favour of the ancient royal name he assumed; perhaps, Ellicott suggests, the Chronicler himself meant to indicate the identity — "the spirit of Pul, that is, the spirit of Tiglath, and he carried them away." Adam Clarke stands between, noting only that many manuscripts read "Tiglath" for "Tilgath," and that the Syriac, Septuagint, and Chaldee agree with the form in 2 Kings 15:29.

On the places of exile the witnesses close the account. The tribes were brought, the verse says, to Halah, and Habor, and Hara, and the river Gozan; Ellicott identifies Habor with a district of northern Assyria named from the river Habur, takes Hara for the mountains of Media, and reads "the river of Gozan" as the same Habur, the region the Assyrians called Guzana. He notes that these are the very places to which Sargon would later transport the western tribes (2 Kings 17:6), and Clarke sends the reader to the fuller history in 2 Kings 17:6 and 2 Kings 18:11, wishing aloud that some means might be found to harmonize the books of Kings with the Chronicles. The witnesses leave the tribes where the Assyrian left them, scattered along the rivers of Media "unto this day." The birthright forfeited in the first verse has become a homeland forfeited in the last; Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh, first to be settled east of Jordan, are first to be swept away — a people who chose the exposed and fertile border, prospered on it, forgot the God who gave it, and vanished from it into the cities of their conquerors.
5:
Set apart at the chapter's close, out of its numerical order, stands the short link that carries the princely line through its middle generations — "Micah his son, Reaia his son, Baal his son." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read it as one segment of the descent from Joel down to Beerah, the prince whom Tiglath-pileser deported; the chain of "his son... his son" is the register's plainest form, a bare succession in which each name simply hands the princedom on to the next. They connect it forward to the brethren of v.7, the fellow-chiefs Jeiel and Zechariah reckoned each after his families in the registration, and back to the founding sons, noting that the word for these "generations" is the same used from Gen. 2:4 onward for the record of a man's posterity.

The one name on which a witness pauses is Baal. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that this element, troubling as it sounds in a covenant genealogy, was in fact common in early Israelite names, and he sets it beside the names of Saul's posterity, Eshbaal and Meribbaal, and David's son Beeliada — proof that the word, meaning simply "lord" or "master," could be borne innocently before it became wholly the name of the false gods against whom the prophets thundered.

The witnesses make of this displaced verse a small emblem of the whole chapter's method and irony. Keil's chain of sons shows the Chronicler doing what he does throughout — keeping the thread of a princely house unbroken, name after unremarkable name, down to the man who lost it all; and Ellicott's note on Baal quietly foreshadows the chapter's end, where these same tribes would go a whoring after the gods whose very name one of their princes had innocently borne. The Reubenite Baal of the pedigree and the Canaanite Baals of the apostasy are worlds apart in meaning, yet the coincidence of the word hangs over the register, a reminder that the tribe named its sons in the fear of the Lord long before it lost them to the idols of the land. Keil's bare chain of sons and Ellicott's single note on a name thus meet in one quiet lesson, that the register keeps its princely thread unbroken even as it hints, in the very name of Baal, at the ruin toward which the tribe was bending.
